researchFindings.claim: Identify a safe place before a tornado strikes: basement, storm cellar, or interior room on the lowest floor with no windows
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core preparedness tip — designating safe place before event

researchFindings.claim: No mobile home is safe in a tornado — residents of mobile or manufactured homes must identify and plan to reach a sturdy nearby building before severe weather season
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Critical warning for mobile home residents

researchFindings.claim: Build a 3-day emergency kit including: water (1 gallon per person per day), non-perishable food, battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA Weather Radio, flashlight, batteries, first aid kit, medications, and copies of important documents
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Emergency kit contents for preparedness guide

researchFindings.claim: Tornado WATCH means a tornado is possible in the area; tornado WARNING means a tornado is already occurring or will occur soon — take shelter immediately
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Critical distinction for preparednessTips and whenToUse

researchFindings.claim: Install a NOAA Weather Radio with battery backup in your home; wireless emergency alerts will be sent automatically to capable smartphones in affected areas
FEMA P-361 and P-320 define criteria for residential safe rooms that provide near-absolute protection in extreme tornado-force winds; safe rooms must resist wind and impact loads per ICC 500
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Safe Rooms for Tornadoes and Hurricanes
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Safe room standards — referenced in preparedness tip on shelter identification

researchFindings.claim: Create a family emergency plan: designate two meeting places, plan for an out-of-town contact, and practice getting to the safe place at least once per year
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Family planning component of preparedness guide

researchFindings.claim: Secure outdoor furniture and decorations before severe weather season; these can become lethal projectiles in tornado-force winds
In a high-rise building, go to a hallway in the center of the building — there may not be enough time to reach the lowest floor
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Safe shelter location for multi-story buildings

researchFindings.claim: Do not shelter in a car or under an overpass during a tornado — both offer no meaningful protection and overpasses can create dangerous wind tunnels

identifiedGaps.area: Specific safe room building materials and costs
identifiedGaps.impact: Useful for homeowners but too granular for a preparedness guide — covered by FEMA P-320
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Omit; reference FEMA safe rooms page for those interested in construction

recommendation: Write a preparedness_guide for the Tornadoes parentTopic. The guide should cover: (1) identify safe shelter location before tornado season, (2) watch vs warning distinction, (3) 3-day emergency kit contents, (4) NOAA Weather Radio setup, (5) family emergency plan with meeting places, (6) mobile home residents must plan to evacuate to sturdy structure, (7) secure outdoor items. Key warnings: mobile homes are never safe in tornadoes; overpasses and cars provide no protection. All claims well-sourced by Red Cross and FEMA.
